input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **Digital Twin** in the provided documents primarily focuses on **cyber-biophysical systems**, particularly within **automotive, manufacturing, aerospace, and battery systems**, as well as broader **industrial IoT** contexts. Key physical twin systems include:

1. **Automotive Systems (Documents [8], [9], [10], [14])**:
   - **Type**: Cyber-physical vehicles, powertrain systems, battery management systems (BMS), and autonomous driving platforms.
   - **Main Components**:
     - **Physical Vehicle**: Sensors (e.g., LiDAR, radar, IMUs), actuators (e.g., throttle, brakes), and embedded control units (ECUs).
     - **Battery Systems (Document [17])**: High-voltage battery packs, thermal management units, and state-of-charge (SoC)/state-of-health (SoH) monitoring hardware.
     - **Manufacturing Systems (Document [10])**: Assembly lines, robotic arms, CNC machines, and supply chain logistics.
   - **Operational Domain**:
     - **Automotive**: On-road (real-time monitoring, predictive maintenance, autonomous navigation) and off-road (e.g., electric vehicle charging infrastructure).
     - **Manufacturing**: Discrete and continuous production lines with real-time quality control and adaptive scheduling.
     - **Aerospace (Document [16])**: On-orbit satellite systems for collision avoidance and space debris detection, integrating propulsion, attitude control, and sensor payloads.
   - **Key Characteristics**:
     - **Dynamic Interconnectivity**: High-frequency data exchange (e.g., 100Hz+ for automotive sensors) between physical and digital domains via **edge computing** (Document [14]).
     - **Multi-Domain Simulation**: Coupled **multi-physics simulations** (thermal, electrical, mechanical) for automotive powertrains (Document [9]).
     - **Real-Time Constraints**: Latency-sensitive operations (e.g., <10ms for autonomous braking systems) requiring **deterministic communication protocols** (e.g., Ethernet TSN, CAN FD).
     - **Scalability**: Support for **systems of twinned systems** (Document [13]), where multiple interconnected twins (e.g., vehicle + charging station + grid) operate as a unified cyber-physical ecosystem.
     - **Federated Architecture**: Decentralized twin instances (e.g., cloud, edge, and fog layers) for **data sovereignty** and **low-latency processing** (Document [14]).
     - **Adaptive Modeling**: Use of **machine learning (ML)** for dynamic parameter calibration (e.g., battery degradation models) and **physics-informed neural networks** (Document [15]).

2. **Cyber-Biophysical Systems (Document [7])**:
   - **Type**: Hybrid systems integrating **biological, physical, and cyber components** (e.g., medical devices, smart agriculture, or industrial bioprocesses).
   - **Key Characteristics**:
     - **Biophysical Coupling**: Real-time bidirectional data flow between **physiological sensors** (e.g., ECG, EEG) and **digital models** (e.g., cardiac electrophysiology simulations).
     - **Uncertainty Quantification**: Incorporation of **probabilistic models** (e.g., Bayesian networks) to handle biological variability.
     - **Regulatory Compliance**: Alignment with **ISO/IEC 30173** (Document [11]) for **safety-critical** applications (e.g., medical implants).

3. **Industrial IoT (Document [12])**:
   - **Type**: Factory automation, predictive maintenance, and asset performance management.
   - **Key Characteristics**:
     - **Heterogeneous Data Integration**: Unified modeling of **discrete-event systems** (e.g., assembly lines) and **continuous processes** (e.g., chemical reactors).
     - **Standardized Interfaces**: Adherence to **ISO 23247** (Document [10]) for **interoperability** across vendors and platforms.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The Digital Twin systems described in the documents provide **specialized services** categorized by **functional domains**, **target users**, and **technical capabilities**. Key services include:

1. **Monitoring Services (Documents [7], [8], [9], [10], [11], [14])**:
   - **Real-Time Telemetry**: Continuous streaming of **sensor data** (e.g., 1kHz+ for automotive powertrains) to the digital twin via **edge gateways** (Document [14]).
   - **Anomaly Detection**: **Machine learning-based** (e.g., LSTM networks) or **rule-based** (e.g., ISO 23247 compliance checks) anomaly detection for **predictive maintenance** (Document [9]).
   - **Health Monitoring**:
     - **Battery Systems (Document [17])**: Real-time SoC/SoH estimation with **thermal and electrical stress modeling**.
     - **Aerospace (Document [16])**: On-orbit collision avoidance using **digital twin simulations** of satellite trajectories and debris clouds.
   - **Target Users**: **Maintenance engineers**, **operations managers**, and **fleet operators**.

2. **Optimization Services (Documents [7], [8], [10], [14], [15])**:
   - **Resource Allocation**: Dynamic optimization of **production schedules** (e.g., mixed-model assembly lines) using **multi-objective optimization** (e.g., MOEA frameworks) (Document [10]).
   - **Energy Management**:
     - **Automotive (Document [8])**: Optimization of **regenerative braking** and **thermal management** for battery efficiency.
     - **Manufacturing (Document [14])**: Edge-based optimization of **energy consumption** in industrial processes.
   - **Trajectory Planning**:
     - **Autonomous Vehicles (Document [8])**: Real-time path optimization using **digital twin simulations** of road conditions and traffic.
     - **Aerospace (Document [16])**: Collision avoidance maneuvers via **digital twin-based trajectory prediction**.
   - **Target Users**: **Supply chain planners**, **energy managers**, and **autonomous vehicle developers**.

3. **Prediction Services (Documents [7], [9], [11], [15])**:
   - **Lifetime Prediction**:
     - **Battery Systems (Document [17])**: **Physics-based degradation models** combined with **ML-driven aging forecasts** (e.g., random forest regression).
     - **Manufacturing Components (Document [9])**: Predictive failure analysis for **rotating machinery** (e.g., bearings, gears) using **vibration signature analysis**.
   - **Performance Forecasting**:
     - **Automotive (Document [8])**: **Fuel consumption** and **emission prediction** under varying driving conditions.
     - **Cyber-Biophysical (Document [7])**: **Physiological response prediction** (e.g., cardiac stress under workload).
   - **Target Users**: **Product designers**, **risk analysts**, and **insurance underwriters**.

4. **Control Services (Documents [7], [8], [14], [16])**:
   - **Closed-Loop Control**:
     - **Automotive (Document [8])**: **Model Predictive Control (MPC)** for powertrain management, integrating digital twin simulations with **real-time ECU updates**.
     - **Aerospace (Document [16])**: **Autonomous collision avoidance** via **digital twin-driven control commands** to satellite thrusters.
   - **Adaptive Control**:
     - **Industrial IoT (Document [12])**: **Self-tuning PID controllers** for robotic arms using digital twin feedback.
   - **Target Users**: **Control systems engineers** and **robotics developers**.

5. **Visualization Services (Documents [10], [11], [15])**:
   - **Immersive Dashboards**: **3D/4D visualization** (time + physics) of **digital twin states** using **Unreal Engine** or **Blender** for **automotive and aerospace applications** (Document [15]).
   - **Augmented Reality (AR)**: **AR overlays** for **maintenance technicians** to visualize **hidden failures** or **optimized assembly paths** (Document [10]).
   - **Target Users**: **Technicians**, **trainers**, and **decision-makers**.

6. **Simulation Services (Documents [7], [9], [10], [15])**:
   - **Multi-Physics Simulation**: **Coupled simulations** of **thermal, electrical, and mechanical domains** (e.g., **COMSOL Multiphysics**, **ANSYS**) for automotive battery packs (Document [9]).
   - **Digital Shadow**: **Real-time shadow simulation** of the physical system for **what-if analysis** (e.g., **ISO 23247-compliant** federated simulations) (Document [10]).
   - **AI-Augmented Simulation**: **Generative AI** for **automated scenario generation** (e.g., **GANs for synthetic crash test data**) (Document [15]).
   - **Target Users**: **Researchers**, **prototyping teams**, and **certification bodies**.

7. **Security and Compliance Services (Documents [11], [12], [14])**:
   - **Data Integrity Verification**: **Blockchain-based** or **hash-based** validation of **digital twin data streams** to prevent tampering (Document [12]).
   - **Regulatory Alignment**: **ISO/IEC 30173** compliance checks for **safety-critical systems** (e.g., medical devices, aerospace) (Document [11]).
   - **Target Users**: **Cybersecurity teams** and **regulatory auditors**.

8. **Interoperability Services (Documents [10], [12], [14])**:
   - **Standardized APIs**: **RESTful/GraphQL APIs** for **cross-platform integration** (e.g., **OPC UA**, **MQTT**) (Document [10]).
   - **Federated Twin Orchestration**: **Edge-cloud hybrid architectures** for **scalable twin deployment** (Document [14]).
   - **Target Users**: **IT architects** and **system integrators**.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The implementation of Digital Twin systems in the provided documents leverages a **heterogeneous stack of tools, frameworks, and enablers**, categorized by **development phases**, **execution environments**, and **specialized technologies**. Key components include:

1. **Development Tools and Frameworks (Documents [7], [10], [12], [14], [15])**:
   - **Modeling Languages**:
     - **SysML** (Systems Modeling Language) for **requirements modeling** and **architecture definition** (Document [10]).
     - **UML** (Unified Modeling Language) for **digital twin ontology** and **interoperability schemas** (Document [11]).
   - **Simulation Engines**:
     - **Multi-Physics**: **COMSOL Multiphysics**, **ANSYS**, and **MATLAB/Simulink** for **automotive powertrain** and **battery thermal modeling** (Documents [9], [17]).
     - **Discrete-Event**: **AnyLogic**, **Simul8**, and **FlexSim** for **manufacturing process simulation** (Document [10]).
     - **Physics-Informed AI**: **TensorFlow Physics** and **PyTorch** for **ML-driven digital twin calibration** (Document [15]).
   - **Digital Twin Platforms**:
     - **Commercial**: **Siemens MindSphere**, **PTC ThingWorx**, **Dassault Systèmes 3DEXPERIENCE**, and **Siemens Digital Twin Creator** (Document [12]).
     - **Open-Source**: **KubeVirt**, **OpenShift**, and **Kubernetes** for **containerized twin deployment** (Document [14]).
   - **Ontology and Semantic Tools**:
     - **OWL/SWRL** for **formal ontology modeling** of digital twin components (Document [11]).
     - **RDF/SPARQL** for **knowledge graph-based querying** of twin data (Document [12]).

2. **Execution Environments (Documents [7], [14], [16])**:
   - **Edge Computing**:
     - **NVIDIA Jetson**, **Intel Edge Insights**, and **AWS IoT Greengrass** for **low-latency processing** of **automotive and industrial IoT data** (Document [14]).
     - **Federated Learning**: **Decentralized AI training** on edge devices to preserve **data privacy** (Document [14]).
   - **Cloud Platforms**:
     - **AWS IoT Core**, **Microsoft Azure Digital Twins**, and **Google Cloud Twin** for **scalable twin orchestration** (Document [12]).
     - **Hybrid Architectures**: **Edge-cloud synergy** via **5G/6G networks** for **ultra-low latency** (Document [14]).
   - **Fog Computing**:
     - **Intermediate processing nodes** (e.g., **Cisco Fog Director**) for **regional data aggregation** in **smart cities** and **industrial zones** (Document [7]).

3. **Databases and Storage (Documents [10], [12], [14])**:
   - **Time-Series Databases**:
     - **InfluxDB**, **TimescaleDB**, and **Prometheus** for **high-frequency sensor data** (e.g., 1kHz automotive telemetry) (Document [14]).
   - **Graph Databases**:
     - **Neo4j**, **ArangoDB** for **relationship-rich twin data** (e.g., **component dependencies** in manufacturing) (Document [12]).
   - **Blockchain**:
     - **Hyperledger Fabric**, **Ethereum** for **immutable twin data logs** and **supply chain traceability** (Document [12]).
   - **Object Storage**:
     - **S3-compatible storage** (e.g., **MinIO**, **AWS S3**) for **large-scale twin asset storage** (e.g., **CAD models**, **simulation outputs**) (Document [10]).

4. **Communication Protocols (Documents [8], [10], [14], [16])**:
   - **Real-Time Protocols**:
     - **Ethernet TSN (Time-Sensitive Networking)** for **deterministic automotive networking** (Document [8]).
     - **CAN FD**, **LIN**, **FlexRay** for **vehicle bus communication** (Document [8]).
     - **MQTT**, **AMQP** for **lightweight IoT messaging** (Document [10]).
   - **Industrial Protocols**:
     - **OPC UA** for **manufacturing system interoperability** (Document [10]).
     - **DDS (Data Distribution Service)** for **high-performance data sharing** in **aerospace and defense** (Document [16]).
   - **5G/6G Enablers**:
     - **Network Slicing** for **QoS differentiation** (e.g., **ultra-reliable low-latency communication (URLLC)** for autonomous vehicles) (Document [14]).

5. **Specialized Technologies (Documents [7], [13], [15], [16])**:
   - **AI/ML Integration**:
     - **Generative AI**: **Diffusion models** for **synthetic twin data augmentation** (Document [15]).
     - **Reinforcement Learning (RL)**: **Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO)** for **autonomous control policies** (Document [8]).
     - **Explainable AI (XAI)**: **SHAP/LIME** for **interpretable twin predictions** (Document [7]).
   - **Digital Twin Standards**:
     - **ISO 23247** (Document [10]): Framework for **manufacturing digital twins**, including **lifecycle management** and **interoperability layers**.
     - **ISO/IEC 30173** (Document [11]): **Conceptual and terminological standards** for digital twins, defining **ontologies**, **data models**, and **service interfaces**.
   - **Systems of Twinned Systems (Document [13])**:
     - **Twinned System Orchestration**: **Microservices-based** architectures for **interconnected twins** (e.g., **vehicle + charging station + grid**).
     - **Federated Twin Management**: **API gateways** (e.g., **Kong**, **Apigee**) for **cross-twin communication**.
   - **Security and Privacy**:
     - **Homomorphic Encryption**: **Secure computation** on **encrypted twin data** (Document [12]).
     - **Federated Learning**: **Privacy-preserving AI training** across multiple twins (Document [14]).
   - **Visualization and AR/VR**:
     - **Unreal Engine 5**, **Unity**, **Blender** for **immersive twin visualization** (Document [15]).
     - **Microsoft HoloLens**, **Meta Quest** for **AR-assisted maintenance** (Document [10]).
   - **Quantum Computing (Mentioned Indirectly)**:
     - **Potential use cases** for **optimization** in **large-scale manufacturing twins** (Document [14]) (not explicitly detailed but implied).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system enforces a **100 ms clock synchronization accuracy** between physical and digital components to enable computation offloading and coordination, adhering to stricter vehicular application standards despite hardware limitations. Temporal granularity is dictated by the ROS2-based execution model, where each Function Entity (FE) is implemented as an individual ROS2 package containing ROS2 nodes. These nodes operate with millisecond-level latency requirements for real-time data processing, actuation, and sensor fusion tasks (e.g., *Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking*). The system’s temporal alignment is critical for synchronizing device communication, pre-processing, and control mechanisms across the *Physical Twin Entity*, *Digital Twin Entity*, and *User Entity* components, ensuring coherent temporal behavior for safety-critical vehicular operations.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system follows a **decentralized, entity-based architecture** with three primary hierarchical instances: the *Physical Twin Entity*, *Digital Twin Entity*, and *User Entity*. Each entity is deployed on distinct hardware components, reflecting a departure from ISO 23247’s standard by consolidating resource-specific and general automotive functionalities (e.g., sensors, actuators, compute resources) within the *Physical Twin Entity*. This design introduces **substantial ownership and engineering process implications**, as functionalities are not strictly aligned with ISO 23247’s Function Entities (FEs). The *User Entity* is further subdivided into *3D Visualization* and *Control Panel* subcomponents, while the *Data Storage* FE lacks a direct FE alignment. Coordination between instances relies on ROS2’s inter-node communication, with no explicit mention of multiple Digital Twin instances or centralized orchestration mechanisms beyond the entity-based decomposition.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system adopts an **ISO 23247 Reference Architecture (RA)-inspired, entity-centric orchestration pattern**, where Function Entities (FEs) are mapped to ROS2 packages and executed as individual ROS2 nodes. The architecture emphasizes **modularity and hardware-specific deployment**, with each entity (*Physical Twin*, *Digital Twin*, *User*) deployed on separate hardware components. Resource management is implicit within the *Physical Twin Entity*, which hosts both resource-specific (e.g., sensors, actuators) and general automotive functionalities (e.g., *Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking*). System-wide coordination is achieved via ROS2’s publish-subscribe model, enabling real-time data exchange and synchronization across entities. The design deviates from ISO 23247 by merging functionalities traditionally separated (e.g., OME and general automotive tasks), impacting functional ownership and engineering workflows.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin system adheres to the **ISO 23247** reference architecture, which defines an **entity-based framework** for manufacturing Digital Twins. The system comprises **Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs)**, including physical entities such as equipment, facilities, products, personnel, and processes. The **Digital Twin Entity** digitally represents each OME using **computational models** that integrate:
- **Geometric models** (for spatial representation of physical assets),
- **Behavioral models** (to simulate operational dynamics),
- **Analytical models** (for performance prediction and optimization).

Data sources are managed via the **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**, which interfaces with OMEs to gather real-time operational data. The system employs **Functional Entities (FEs)**—modular components responsible for specific tasks such as data transformation, human-interpretable formatting, or user interaction. While the standard does not prescribe implementation details, **OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA)** is referenced as a potential protocol for interoperability between entities. Model relationships are established through **entity-based decomposition**, where each OME’s Digital Twin Entity interacts with its corresponding Data Collection Entity and User Entity to ensure synchronized data flow and functionality.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components:
The **physical acting components** in this Digital Twin system are governed by the **Data Collection and Device Control Entity** as per **ISO 23247**. This entity is responsible for **remote control mechanisms** and **actuation capabilities** over Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs). While specific actuator types, ranges, or safety constraints are not detailed in the provided documents, the architecture implies:
- **Remote control interfaces** for OMEs (e.g., equipment, facilities) via standardized protocols (e.g., **OPC UA**),
- **Functional Entities (FEs)** for actuating OMEs, though implementation specifics (e.g., PID controllers, motor drivers) are not specified,
- **Safety constraints** are inferred to be managed through the entity-based framework, ensuring controlled interactions between Digital Twin Entities and physical systems.

No explicit details on actuation ranges, limitations, or addressing mechanisms are provided in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components:
The **physical sensing infrastructure** is managed by the **Data Collection and Device Control Entity** within the **ISO 23247** framework. While the documents do not specify:
- **Sensor types** (e.g., IoT sensors, industrial sensors),
- **Measurement capabilities** (e.g., temperature, vibration, pressure),
- **Spatial distribution** (e.g., sensor placement strategies),
- **Sampling frequencies** or **data transmission rates**,
- **Accuracy/precision specifications** (e.g., ±X% error margins),

the architecture implies that sensors are deployed to collect **observable data** from OMEs (e.g., equipment, processes) for real-time monitoring. Data collection mechanisms are abstracted through the **Data Collection Entity**, which interfaces with sensors and transmits data to the **Digital Twin Entity** for processing. No quantitative details (e.g., Hz sampling rates, latency thresholds) are provided.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
The **ISO 23247** standard outlines foundational principles for **model fidelity** and **validity** but does not provide explicit details on:
- **Validation/Verification (V&V) methods** (e.g., simulation testing, regression analysis),
- **Uncertainty quantification** (e.g., statistical error margins, confidence intervals),
- **Error detection/correction mechanisms** (e.g., anomaly detection algorithms),
- **Quality assurance processes** (e.g., compliance checks, auditing protocols).

However, the entity-based architecture implies that **Functional Entities (FEs)** may incorporate fidelity checks (e.g., data consistency validation) and that **Digital Twin Entities** must align with real-world OME behavior to ensure **observable accuracy**. No technical specifications (e.g., tolerance thresholds, validation frameworks) are mentioned in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The **Physical-to-Virtual Interaction** in this Digital Twin System (DTS) involves real-time data acquisition from physical sensors (LiDAR, motor, steering servo, and vehicle state sensors) and their transformation into digital representations. **Data types transmitted** include raw point cloud data (LiDAR), vehicle state metrics (e.g., motor actuation, steering servo angles), and point cloud clustering/tracking outputs. **Preprocessing steps** are performed by the **Data Pre-Processing Functional Entity (FE)**, which clusters and tracks raw LiDAR point clouds (initially represented as 2D points) into structured object tracks and driving path projections. **Communication protocols** for data transmission are not explicitly detailed, but the system relies on **ROS2** for intra-entity communication and **Wi-Fi with UDP** for inter-entity data propagation. **Transmission frequencies** are implied to be high (real-time), as the system displays **vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path** in real-time for **ACC (Adaptive Cruise Control)** monitoring (referenced in Fig. 3a). **Data quality assurance** is inferred through the **Data Translation FE**, which serializes data, aligns message types, and performs **unit conversions** to ensure consistency across data streams. **Integration mechanisms** include alignment of sensor data with the **3D Visualization FE**, which presents processed data to users via a **virtual cockpit interface** (Fig. 3a/b).
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The **Virtual-to-Physical Interaction** in this DTS is facilitated by the **Controlling FE**, which executes commands derived from digital twin operations (e.g., ACC logic) and transmits them to physical actuators. **Command types** include **control signals** for motor actuation and steering servo adjustments, formatted via the **Data Translation FE** for serialization and unit alignment. **Validation and authentication mechanisms** are not explicitly described, but the system relies on **ROS2** for intra-component communication and **Wi-Fi/UDP** for inter-entity control signal transmission, implying protocol-level integrity checks. **Feedback systems** are implied through the **Data Collecting FE**, which captures real-time vehicle state data post-actuation, enabling closed-loop control. **Conflict resolution** is not detailed, but the system’s reliance on **ACC-specific data** (e.g., object tracks, driving path projections) suggests prioritization of safety-critical commands. **Command execution confirmation** is inferred via the **Reporting FE**, which generates reports on system performance and actuator responses.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **Digital Twin’s Technical Connection** infrastructure is built on a **multi-layered network architecture**:
- **Intra-entity communication** occurs via **ROS2**, a middleware framework enabling real-time data exchange between software components (e.g., Data Pre-Processing FE, Controlling FE).
- **Inter-entity communication** uses **Wi-Fi with UDP** at the transport layer, ensuring low-latency, broadcast-capable data transmission between entities (e.g., Physical Twin Entity ↔ Digital Twin Entity).
- **Bandwidth and latency requirements** are not explicitly quantified, but the system’s reliance on **real-time 3D visualization** (Fig. 3a) and **ACC logic** implies stringent latency constraints (<100ms for control loops). **Reliability mechanisms** are not detailed, but redundancy is implied through the **Data Translation FE**, which standardizes data formats across heterogeneous streams.
- **Security protocols** are not mentioned, though UDP’s lack of built-in encryption suggests potential vulnerabilities. **Network topology** appears decentralized, with entities communicating peer-to-peer via Wi-Fi, while ROS2 handles internal component orchestration.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **Digital Twin Hosting and Deployment** infrastructure is not explicitly detailed in the provided documents. Key assumptions based on context:
- **Hosting platforms**: The system’s reliance on **ROS2** and **Wi-Fi/UDP** suggests an **edge computing deployment**, where processing occurs locally (e.g., on-board vehicle systems) rather than in the cloud. **On-premise deployment** is implied for the Physical Twin Entity (e.g., vehicle sensors and actuators).
- **Computational requirements**: High-performance processing is required for **LiDAR point cloud clustering/tracking** and **real-time 3D visualization**, likely necessitating embedded systems with GPU acceleration (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson for edge AI).
- **Storage requirements**: The **Reporting FE** generates data storage for reports, implying persistent storage for logs and analytics, though specifics (e.g., capacity, database type) are not provided.
- **Scalability mechanisms**: Not described, but the modular **Functional Entity (FE)** architecture suggests potential for horizontal scaling via ROS2’s distributed computing capabilities.
- **Availability/reliability assurance**: No explicit mechanisms (e.g., failover, backup) are mentioned, though the **Data Translation FE**’s role in data serialization may mitigate inconsistencies.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
The lifecycle of the Digital Twin (DT) system adheres to the **ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manufacturing** standard, structured into distinct phases aligned with its **entity-based reference architecture (RA)**. The lifecycle begins with **development**, where **Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs)**—such as equipment, facilities, or processes—are identified and mapped to their corresponding **Digital Twin Entities (DTEs)**. Each OME is represented digitally via the DTE, while **Data Collection and Device Control Entities (DCCEs)** handle real-time data acquisition and actuation, ensuring bidirectional synchronization between physical and digital domains.

Representation types evolve through **phases of abstraction**:
1. **Conceptual Phase**: Defines OMEs and their functional requirements, using **Functional Entities (FEs)** to modularize tasks (e.g., data transformation, user interfacing). The standard does not prescribe implementation tools, but **OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA)** is implied as a viable protocol for interoperability.
2. **Implementation Phase**: Deploys DTEs with DCCEs, integrating sensors/actuators for real-time monitoring and control. Transition management involves **entity composition**, where FEs are linked to OMEs via standardized interfaces (e.g., OPC UA information models).
3. **Operational Phase**: The DT operates in a **closed-loop feedback system**, where DTEs update based on DCCE-collected data, and user interactions via **User Entities (UEs)** enable monitoring/control. Lifecycle governance follows **ISO 23247’s modular RA**, ensuring scalability through entity-based updates (e.g., replacing a DCCE without disrupting the DTE).

Lifecycle governance emphasizes **entity autonomy**: Each entity (OME, DCCE, DTE, UE) operates independently but collaborates via standardized interfaces, enabling incremental evolution (e.g., adding new FEs for analytics without redesigning the entire DT). The standard does not specify governance frameworks (e.g., ISO 55000 for asset management) but aligns with **modular, iterative development** principles.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The **twinning process** is governed by the **ISO 23247 reference architecture**, which mandates a **modular, entity-driven development methodology**. The process begins with **requirement elicitation** for OMEs (e.g., machinery, processes), where each OME is assigned a **Digital Twin Entity (DTE)** for digital representation. **Data Collection and Device Control Entities (DCCEs)** are deployed to interface with physical sensors/actuators, ensuring real-time data flow via **bidirectional communication protocols** (e.g., OPC UA, implied by the standard’s flexibility).

**Quality assurance (QA)** is implicit in the **entity-based RA**: Each entity (OME, DCCE, DTE, UE) undergoes **functional validation** to ensure correct implementation of its designated tasks (e.g., DCCEs must accurately collect/transmit data; DTEs must mirror OME states). The standard does not prescribe QA tools but emphasizes **modular testing**—validating FEs (e.g., data transformation logic) independently before integration. **Version control** is inferred through the RA’s **entity autonomy**: Updates to individual entities (e.g., replacing a DCCE) are managed via standardized interfaces, preventing cascading failures.

**Evolution management** follows **incremental, entity-centric updates**: New FEs (e.g., predictive maintenance algorithms) can be added to DTEs without redesigning the entire DT. The standard does not mandate specific versioning systems but supports **backward compatibility** through modular design. **Testing and validation** are iterative, aligning with the RA’s **closed-loop feedback** principle, where DTEs are continuously validated against OME data streams. The process lacks explicit mention of CI/CD pipelines but aligns with **agile, iterative development** for DTs.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The DT system’s **decision support capabilities** are derived from its **entity-based RA**, enabling **real-time analytics and user-driven insights**. **Digital Twin Entities (DTEs)** process data collected by **Data Collection and Device Control Entities (DCCEs)**, generating **time-series and state-based insights** (e.g., equipment health, process efficiency). The standard does not specify analytics tools but implies **modular processing**: **Functional Entities (FEs)** within DTEs can include algorithms for data transformation (e.g., normalization) or feature extraction (e.g., anomaly detection).

**Decision-making processes** rely on **user interactions via User Entities (UEs)**, which provide interfaces for monitoring (e.g., dashboards) and control (e.g., actuator commands). The RA supports **confidence measures** through **entity synchronization**: DTEs update dynamically based on DCCE data, ensuring insights reflect the latest physical state. However, the standard does not detail **confidence metrics** (e.g., uncertainty quantification) or **recommendation algorithms** (e.g., reinforcement learning).

**Analytics capabilities** are constrained by the RA’s **modularity**: FEs can be extended for advanced processing (e.g., machine learning), but the standard does not prescribe methods. **User interaction methods** are UE-dependent, with examples including **OPC UA-based visualization** (implied) or custom interfaces. The system lacks explicit **decision automation** (e.g., autonomous control) but enables **human-in-the-loop** decisions via UE feedback loops.
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
The DT system adheres to the **ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manufacturing** standard, which defines a **comprehensive compliance framework** for manufacturing DTs. The standard is divided into four parts, with **Part 2 (Reference Architecture)** being critical: it mandates an **entity-based RA** where **Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs)** are digitally represented by **Digital Twin Entities (DTEs)**, with **Data Collection and Device Control Entities (DCCEs)** handling physical interaction and **User Entities (UEs)** enabling human-machine interfaces.

**Relevant industry standards** include:
- **ISO 23247 (2021)**: Defines the RA, entity roles, and information exchange principles. It does not prescribe specific technologies but emphasizes **modularity** and **interoperability** via standardized interfaces.
- **OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA)**: Implied as a viable protocol for **interoperability** between entities (e.g., DCCEs and DTEs), though not explicitly mandated by ISO 23247.
- **No explicit certification requirements** are mentioned in the standard, but compliance with ISO 23247 ensures **structural adherence** to the RA.

**Interoperability standards** are inferred from the RA’s **entity autonomy**: Each entity communicates via **standardized interfaces** (e.g., OPC UA information models), enabling seamless integration across heterogeneous systems. The standard does not reference **other interoperability frameworks** (e.g., ROS, IIoT protocols) but prioritizes **modular, plug-and-play compatibility** between entities.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations:
The security and safety considerations for the Digital Twin system, as referenced in the provided documents, are primarily governed by **ISO 26262-1:2018** for functional safety in road vehicles and **ISO 15622:2018** for adaptive cruise control systems. Key technical measures include:

1. **Functional Safety (ISO 26262-1:2018)**:
   - **Automotive Safety Integrity Levels (ASILs)**: The system must adhere to ASIL decomposition to ensure safety requirements are met, with risk assessment conducted using **ISO 26262-5** methodologies (though not explicitly referenced here, implied by the standard’s framework).
   - **Fail-Safe Mechanisms**: Design principles mandate fail-safe behaviors for critical components, such as **emergency stop systems** or **redundant control pathways**, to mitigate hardware/software failures.
   - **Safety Lifecycle (ISO 26262-3)**: The system must undergo **safety planning, hazard analysis (e.g., FMEA/FMEDA), and safety validation** at each development phase, with traceability to safety goals.

2. **Cybersecurity Protocols (Implied by ISO 15622 and 3GPP TS 22.185)**:
   - **Authentication and Access Control**: The system must enforce **role-based access control (RBAC)** for V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) services, as outlined in **3GPP TS 22.185**, ensuring only authorized entities (e.g., vehicles, infrastructure) interact with the Digital Twin.
   - **Threat Mitigation**: **Encryption (e.g., AES-128/256)** and **message authentication codes (MACs)** are required for secure communication channels, per **ISO 15622**’s performance requirements for adaptive cruise control systems.
   - **Risk Assessment**: **ISO 26262-9** (though not cited) would dictate **cybersecurity risk analysis** aligned with ASIL, focusing on attack surfaces like **unauthorized data access** or **malicious input manipulation**.

3. **Safety-Critical System Considerations**:
   - **Hardware/Software Partitioning**: Critical functions (e.g., collision avoidance logic) must operate in **isolated partitions** to prevent cascading failures, adhering to **ISO 26262-6**’s guidelines for hardware and software safety.
   - **Real-Time Constraints**: The system must guarantee **deterministic response times** (e.g., <100ms for emergency braking) as specified in **ISO 15622** for adaptive cruise control performance.
   - **Validation and Testing**: **Fault injection testing** and **hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) simulations** are mandated to verify fail-safe behaviors under **ISO 26262-7**’s test case specifications.

4. **Model-Driven Safety (Dalibor et al., 2020)**:
   - The **interactive Digital Twin cockpit** architecture (per [37]) emphasizes **model-driven engineering (MDE)** for safety-critical components, ensuring **automated compliance checks** against safety requirements via **UML/SysML** or **Simulink** models.
   - **Safety-by-Design**: The Digital Twin’s **interactive visualization** must include **real-time safety alerts** (e.g., ASIL-declared warnings) to operators, aligning with **ISO 26262-8**’s human-machine interface (HMI) guidelines.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

(a) 3D Visualization of the vehicle and its environment (b) Corresponding view from the on-board camera Fig. 3: Visualizations available from the virtual cockpit _C. Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a. Raw point cloud data from the LiDAR sensor must be processed by the _Point Cloud_ _Clustering & Tracking_ SW component, which turns the raw point cloud, represented as individual points on a 2D plane,
